articles.title: Public health faces longevity imperative
articles.summary: A new Longevity Show commentary suggests that treating geroscience as a future luxury is an administrative gamble with a looming expiry date. While longevity science quietly secures its foothold in policy circles, public health infrastructure remains largely frozen in a reactive posture. The equation is simple enough, yet a new commentary from The Longevity Show […] The post Public health faces longevity imperative appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Beyond single targets
articles.summary: Patented MT Company’s Volodymyr Naumenko explains why the future of longevity medicine demands a systems engineering perspective. The longevity field is accelerating, rapidly. Recent leaps in multi-omic biomarkers, computational biology and precision medicine have shifted the paradigm entirely. Aging is no longer viewed as some immutable consequence of the clock; it is a malleable biological […] The post Beyond single targets appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Harvard Publishes a Longevity Report for the General Public
articles.summary: The report, titled “Pathways to Longevity”, introduces several important longevity concepts to the general reader and is another sign that the field is coming of age and entering the mainstream. People do want to live longer From time to […]

articles.title: Lithuania takes longevity into parliament
articles.summary: The Healthy Ageing and Longevity Assembly 2026 aims to connect geroscience, prevention and policy across an entire nation. Lithuania is preparing to host what may prove to be one of the more politically ambitious longevity gatherings of the year; not because of celebrity speakers or investment fanfare, but because the country appears intent on treating […] The post Lithuania takes longevity into parliament appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: What poor sleep may be telling women about Alzheimer’s?
articles.summary: New research suggests sleep complaints in older women may be tied to early Alzheimer’s-related brain changes. A particular kind of exhaustion many older women know well is waking up at 3am, staring at the ceiling for an hour, drifting back to sleep just before dawn, then spending the next day brushing it off as part […] The post What poor sleep may be telling women about Alzheimer’s? appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Scribe reports up to 68% LDL-C reduction with single-dose epigenetic therapy
articles.summary: Scribe Therapeutics presented late-breaking preclinical data at the 94th European Atherosclerosis Society Congress showing that a single dose of an STX-1150 prototype produced ldl-c reductions up to 68% and pcsk9 reductions up to about 90% in non-human primates, with the lowest dose yielding greater than 50% ldl-c reduction for more than 22 months and ongoing. […] The post Scribe reports up to 68% LDL-C reduction with single-dose epigenetic therapy appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Human Longevity Inc. launches new entity to build AI health models
articles.summary: Human Longevity, Inc. announced the creation of Human Life Foundation Models, Inc. (HLFM), a new entity focused on developing large multimodal foundation models for human health and longevity. HLFM’s first strategic collaboration is a multi-year, multi-million dollar agreement with Insilico Medicine to combine Human Longevity’s de-identified multi-omic, imaging, clinical and longitudinal datasets with Insilico Medicine’s […] The post Human Longevity Inc. launches new entity to build AI health models appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Verge Labs launches as frontier AI lab for disease biology, brain health
articles.summary: On 27 May 2026, Verge Labs, formerly Verge Genomics, launched as a frontier AI lab focused on building foundation models of human disease biology and accelerating CNS drug development. Headquartered in San Francisco and founded in 2015, Verge is training generative AI models on VergeDB, its proprietary multimodal human brain dataset; according to the company, […] The post Verge Labs launches as frontier AI lab for disease biology, brain health appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Ribo seeks EU approval for Phase 2 trial of new heart disease drug
articles.summary: Ribo has submitted a Phase 2 clinical trial application to the European Medicines Agency for RBD1119, a small interfering RNA therapeutic candidate for coronary artery disease, the company announced 28 May 2026. The filing, made by Suzhou Ribo Life Science and Ribocure Pharmaceuticals, advances RBD1119 into the next stage of clinical development in Europe and […] The post Ribo seeks EU approval for Phase 2 trial of new heart disease drug appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Freeze-dried reagents and hand-powered hardware bring biomanufacturing to remote labs
articles.summary: Researchers at the University of Toronto's Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy, working with collaborators around the world, have demonstrated the effectiveness of a suite of low-cost, portable biotechnology tools designed to improve access to laboratory research and diagnostics in resource-limited settings.

articles.title: Optically detected and radio wave-controlled spin chemistry in flavoproteins
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 29 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03158-5 Radio waves are shown to modulate fluorescence and associate spin chemistry in proteins.

articles.title: AI and drones can help improve early warning systems for Vibrio bacteria in the Baltic Sea
articles.summary: The presence of the marine bacterium Vibrio vulnificus, which is potentially dangerous to humans, can now be predicted up to five weeks in advance in the Baltic Sea using artificial intelligence (AI). A research team led by the Leibniz Institute for Baltic Sea Research Warnemünde (IOW) has reached this conclusion by combining high-resolution environmental, satellite, and microbiome data in an AI-based analysis.

articles.title: Nitrogen-fixing genes moved into new bacterial strains, opening path beyond fertilizer
articles.summary: Most major crops, such as wheat and corn, require expensive nitrogen fertilizer to flourish. But what if bacteria could help those plants draw nitrogen from the atmosphere, as peas and beans do?

articles.title: Genetically engineering cyanobacteria for the production of sulfated polysaccharide
articles.summary: Biomolecules are naturally occurring molecules that form the basis of living systems. They are widely used in the production of a diverse range of materials. One such widely used biomolecule is sulfated polysaccharide (SPS), which includes sugar molecules attached to sulfate groups. These are widely used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and functional materials due to their unique physical and biological properties. However, commercially available SPSs are usually derived from animal or marine sources, which raises concerns about their environmental impact, calling for alternative SPS production methods.

articles.title: Improved embryo freezing technique could preserve endangered species
articles.summary: The current practice of freezing embryos—used to assist reproduction in humans or animals or to conserve endangered species—routinely causes ice to form within the cells, ripping through cell membranes, changing the way proteins behave and ultimately leading to fewer viable embryos.

articles.title: Electrical pulses reverse aging in sea squirts, offering clues for extending human longevity
articles.summary: A tiny sea creature might hold the secret to reversing the aging process. When treated with a brief series of electrical pulses, sea squirts experience dramatic and long-lasting health improvements that can significantly extend their lifespans, according to a new study by researchers at Stanford and other institutions.

articles.title: Platform fast-tracks microbial design for high-temp manufacturing
articles.summary: Scientists at the Department of Energy's Oak Ridge National Laboratory have developed a platform that engineers heat-loving microbes for industrial-scale manufacturing in a matter of weeks compared with previous approaches that can take months to years. This biotechnology tool strengthens American manufacturing capacity, supporting long-term energy security and global competitiveness.

articles.title: DNA 'nicks' make for safer, more precise genetic analysis
articles.summary: Researchers at Cornell University have developed a safer and more precise way to study how genes function in living tissues by refining a recently developed CRISPR-based genetic technique in fruit flies, enabling researchers to better study how genes contribute to development and disease.

articles.title: Sea squirt reveals glowing spines and unexpected nervous system anatomy
articles.summary: Ascidians, also known as sea squirts, are the evolutionary link between vertebrates and invertebrates, making them valuable subjects of biological studies. For the first time, researchers at Ruhr University Bochum have detected pronounced autofluorescence in Halocynthia papillosa, one of more than 3,000 species. Furthermore, the study provides comprehensive insights into the anatomy of this sea squirt.

articles.title: U.S. says it seized about $1 billion in Iranian crypto as pressure campaign expands
articles.summary: The seizure, falling under "Operation Economic Fury," aims to restrict Iran's access to overseas revenue, banking networks, and crypto infrastructure.

articles.title: Wall Street’s trillion-dollar dilemma: Why AI-powered hackers are keeping big banks off the blockchain
articles.summary: CertiK CEO and co-founder Ronghui Gu says April was the worst month for DeFi in four years with exploits on 27 out of 30 days.

articles.title: SEC charges Texas man with $12.3M crypto fraud using fake AI trading bots
articles.summary: The SEC charged Texas man Nathan Fuller with raising $12.3 million from 150 investors through a crypto fraud scheme built around fake AI trading bots.

articles.title: Here’s what happened in crypto today
articles.summary: Need to know what happened in crypto today? Here is the latest news on daily trends and events impacting Bitcoin price, blockchain, DeFi, Web3 and crypto regulation.

articles.title: When the market is bad, we build: Inside Binance’s bold 2030 master plan
articles.summary: Established crypto firms will merge with traditional finance, but neither Wall Street bankers nor corporate giants will take over the crypto industry, said Binance’s Head of VIP and Institutional, Catherine Chen.

articles.title: Hyperliquid could become a ‘financial services juggernaut’ as DeFi expands, says Grayscale
articles.summary: The digital asset manager says Hyperliquid is emerging as a blockchain-based financial infrastructure platform with the potential to expand beyond crypto trading and challenge parts of traditional derivatives and exchange markets.

articles.title: Court-ordered Circle freeze traps $12.6 million in Zama cUSDC contract amid Overnight Finance suit
articles.summary: Zama CEO Rand Hindi said the protocol's confidential USDC contract was "caught in a crossfire" and that his team is investigating the freeze.

articles.title: XRP ETFs add $35 million as bitcoin and ether funds lost $2 billion in late May
articles.summary: From May 20 to May 29, XRP funds took in $35 million while bitcoin and ether ETFs lost roughly $2 billion combined, with Ripple’s earlier reported XRP treasury plan still awaiting confirmation.

articles.title: Spot Bitcoin ETFs see record 10-day outflow streak, analyst calls it ‘contrarian indicator’
articles.summary: Spot Bitcoin ETFs logged a record 10-day outflow streak totaling nearly $3 billion, while Ether ETFs bled for 14 consecutive sessions.

articles.title: Bitcoin treasury space still has fair share of ‘carnival barkers’: BSTR founder
articles.summary: BSTR co-founder Sean Bill says many Bitcoin treasury companies lack the “ability to actually deploy Bitcoin.”
